messages.content: fix this body paragraph shane kyoczan never says anything about statistics in "to this day" and make a transition into ethos:In “To This Day,” Shane Koyczan employs logos, appealing to the audience’s logic and reasoning to emphasize the pervasiveness and severity of bullying. Throughout his presentation, he cites alarming statistics that highlight the extent of the issue, such as “one in seven students experience bullying” and “160,000 students stay home from school every day due to the fear of being bullied.” These figures serve not only to emphasize the magnitude of the problem but also to awaken the audience to the stark reality faced by countless individuals. Beyond the facts, Koyczan utilizes powerful metaphors and descriptive language to convey the emotional cost and lifelong impact of bullying on victims, illustrating the complex nature of the problem rationally and making it difficult for the audience to disregard. As he discusses the profound effects of bullying, including its impact on mental health, self-esteem, and social relationships, Koyczan highlights the need for immediate action to address the issue thoughtfully and systematically. Through his incorporation of logos, Koyczan effectively underscores the urgency of confronting bullying and encourages the audience to support meaningful change.

In “To This Day,” Shane Koyczan masterfully employs ethos and logos to emphasize the pervasiveness and severity of bullying. As a spoken word artist with a powerful personal experience, Koyczan expertly establishes his credibility and displays his profound understanding of the topic. He expertly captures the audience's attention and empathy by sharing his personal journey through bullying and its impact on his life. By doing so, Koyczan connects with his audience on a deeper level and appeals to their sense of humanity and empathy, while also demonstrating his sincerity towards the subject matter.

Beyond his personal account, Koyczan also employs logos by utilizing powerful metaphors and descriptive language, appealing to the audience’s logic and reasoning. He conveys the emotional cost and lifelong impact of bullying on victims, illustrating the complex nature of the problem rationally and making it difficult for the audience to disregard. As he discusses the profound effects of bullying, including its impact on mental health, self-esteem, and social relationships, Koyczan highlights the need for immediate action to address the issue thoughtfully and systematically.

Through his incorporation of ethos and logos, Koyczan effectively underscores the urgency of confronting bullying and encourages the audience to support meaningful change. By combining the personal aspect of emotional appeal with the rational perspective of logical reasoning, Koyczan's presentation both grips the heart and convinces the mind, creating a compelling argument for the eradication of bullying in our society.
make it one whole body paragraph

In “To This Day,” Shane Koyczan skillfully employs logos to emphasize the pervasiveness and severity of bullying, appealing to the audience’s logic and reasoning. He utilizes powerful metaphors and descriptive language to convey the emotional cost and lifelong impact of bullying on victims, illustrating the complex nature of the problem rationally and making it difficult for the audience to disregard. Through his discussion of the profound effects of bullying—including its impact on mental health, self-esteem, and social relationships—Koyczan highlights the need for immediate action to thoughtfully and systematically address the issue. By capturing the audience's attention and engaging their intellect, he successfully underscores the urgency of confronting bullying and encourages support for meaningful change. As he combines the intellectual weight of logical reasoning with the emotional depth of his own personal experience, Koyczan sets the stage for a seamless transition into an exploration of ethos in the following paragraph.

In “To This Day,” Shane Koyczan skillfully employs logos to emphasize the pervasiveness and severity of bullying, appealing to the audience’s logic and reasoning. He utilizes powerful metaphors and descriptive language to convey the emotional cost and lifelong impact of bullying on victims, illustrating the complex nature of the problem rationally and making it difficult for the audience to disregard. For instance, Koyczan poignantly states, "We were freaks...but that was long before we became something to hold" (2:38-2:47), emphasizing the lifelong impact of bullying and how victims carry those experiences throughout their lives. Through his discussion of the profound effects of bullying—including its impact on mental health, self-esteem, and social relationships—Koyczan highlights the need for immediate action to thoughtfully and systematically address the issue. By capturing the audience's attention and engaging their intellect, he successfully underscores the urgency of confronting bullying and encourages support for meaningful change. As he combines the intellectual weight of logical reasoning with the emotional depth of his own personal experience, Koyczan sets the stage for a seamless transition into an exploration of ethos in the following paragraph.

In “To This Day,” Shane Koyczan skillfully employs logos to emphasize the pervasiveness and severity of bullying, appealing to the audience’s logic and reasoning. He utilizes powerful metaphors and descriptive language to convey the emotional cost and lifelong impact of bullying on victims, illustrating the complex nature of the problem rationally and making it difficult for the audience to disregard. For instance, Koyczan poignantly states, "We are graduating members from the class of 'we made it,' not the faded echoes of voices crying out, 'names will never hurt me'" (6:30-6:41), emphasizing that overcoming the effects of bullying is a continuous process, as the memories still linger. Through his discussion of the profound effects of bullying—including its impact on mental health, self-esteem, and social relationships—Koyczan highlights the need for immediate action to thoughtfully and systematically address the issue. By capturing the audience's attention and engaging their intellect, he successfully underscores the urgency of confronting bullying and encourages support for meaningful change. In “To This Day,” Shane Koyczan skillfully employs logos to emphasize the pervasiveness and severity of bullying, appealing to the audience’s logic and reasoning. He uses powerful metaphors and descriptive language to convey the emotional cost and lifelong impact of bullying on victims, illustrating the complex nature of the problem rationally and making it difficult for the audience to disregard. For instance, Koyczan states, "And as we grew up, we told ourselves we would choose something more realistic to be when we grew up, but... do we know what that even means?" (5:31-5:39), highlighting the detrimental effect bullying has on an individual's aspirations and self-confidence, causing them to question and doubt their potential. Through his discussion of the profound effects of bullying—including its impact on mental health, self-esteem, and social relationships—Koyczan underlines the need for immediate action to thoughtfully and systematically address the issue. By capturing the audience's attention and engaging their intellect, he successfully underscores the urgency of confronting bullying and encourages support for meaningful change. As he combines the intellectual weight of logical reasoning with the emotional depth of his own personal experience, Koyczan sets the stage for a seamless transition into an exploration of ethos in the following paragraph.
when does he mention broken glass and splinterred hearts

Shane Koyczan mentions broken glass and splintered hearts in his poem, "To This Day," during the lines "We are graduating members from the class of 'We Made It,' not the faded echoes of voices crying out 'names will never hurt me.' Of course, they did. But our lives will only ever always continue to be a balancing act that has less to do with pain and more to do with beauty. Like the way the constellations trapped inside broken glass and splintered hearts are proof that we may have been born of tragedy, but we are not tragedy" (6:30-7:15). Here, Koyczan emphasizes the resilience of individuals who faced bullying, and that while their past experiences may have been painful, they emerge stronger and strive for a balanced life filled with beauty, like constellations shining despite the brokenness.

fix this because he never mentions splintered hearts and broken glass: One of the most powerful elements of Koyczan’s poem is its appeal to pathos, as he evokes strong emotions in his audience from the vivid imagery to the raw, painful emotions of his performance. He paints a picture of the relentless torment faced by victims of bullying, describing the “broken glass and splintered hearts” left in the wake of their suffering. Koyczan also addresses the feelings of hopelessness and despair felt by those who have been bullied, reminding them that “our lives will only ever always continue to be a balancing act that has less to do with pain and more to do with beauty.”(10:01) By invoking such intense emotion, Koyczan captures the audience’s attention and sympathy, creating a deeper connection to the subject matter.

One of the most powerful elements of Koyczan’s poem is its appeal to pathos, as he evokes strong emotions in his audience through vivid imagery and the raw, painful emotions conveyed through his performance. He paints a picture of the relentless torment faced by victims of bullying, creating a metaphor of the pain as "constellations trapped inside broken glass" left in the wake of their suffering. Koyczan also addresses the feelings of hopelessness and despair felt by those who have been bullied, reminding them that “our lives will only ever always continue to be a balancing act that has less to do with pain and more to do with beauty” (6:57-7:07). By invoking such intense emotion, Koyczan captures the audience’s attention and sympathy, creating a deeper connection to the subject matter.

Koyczan also addresses the feelings of hopelessness and despair felt by those who have been bullied, reminding them that “our lives will only ever always continue to be a balancing act that has less to do with pain and more to do with beauty” (6:57-7:07). By invoking such intense emotion, Koyczan captures the audience’s attention and sympathy, creating a deeper connection to the subject matter.
